expectations.text: Uses 'hire' and/or 'fire' language to describe how customers choose or abandon products
Names or clearly invokes the three dimensions of a job (functional, emotional, social)
expectations.evidence: Explicit table header 'The Three Dimensions of This Job' with rows for Functional, Emotional, and Social, each with detailed descriptions.

expectations.text: Frames the job in terms of circumstances/context, not customer demographics or attributes
expectations.evidence: All four candidate job statements use 'When [circumstances]' format (e.g., 'When I'm walking into a major gift meeting,' 'When my board and staff each describe what we do in five different ways'). No demographic framing.

expectations.text: Explicitly moves beyond the functional job ('produce a video') to emotional/social dimensions
expectations.evidence: Opens with 'You're not being paid to make a video.' Emotional dimension includes 'Feel proud,' 'Stop feeling embarrassed,' 'Relieve the private anxiety.' Social dimension includes 'Be perceived by the board as a competent leader,' 'Be perceived by funders as professional.'

expectations.text: Describes the job as progress from one state to another in specific circumstances, not as a deliverable
expectations.evidence: Four job statements all follow the progress template 'When [circumstances], I want to [progress], so I can [outcome]' — e.g., 'When I'm walking into a major gift meeting... I want a 2-minute asset that does the emotional heavy lifting... so I can spend the meeting on the ask instead of re-explaining who we are.'
